translations.source: Records of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
translations.source_text: 時充國年七十餘，上老之，使御史大夫丙吉問：「誰可將者？」充國對曰：「亡踰於老臣者矣。」上遣問焉，曰：「將軍度羌虜何如，當用幾人？」充國曰：「百聞不如一見。兵難隃度，臣願馳至金城，圖上方略。然羌戎小夷，逆天背畔，滅亡不久，願陛下以屬老臣，勿以為憂。」上笑曰：「諾。」
translations.translation:
Li Hui (De’ang)
李恢 (德昂)
Lived AD ?–231
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
byZL181
onMarch 28th, 2025

Li Hui, styledDe’ang, was fromYuyuan[俞元],Jianning[建寧].
He served his commandery asDistrict Inspector. His paternal uncle-in-law,Cuan Xi, served asPrefectofJianling[建伶令], and committed some illegal deeds.[Li] Huiwas to be implicated [of his association with][Cuan] Xiand removed from office, butGrand AdministratorDong He, because[Cuan] Xiwas of a major clan of the area, overlooked and did not approve of such,Aand he would later recommended[Li] Huito the province. He was on the road but had not yet arrived when he heard that theFormer LordattackedLiu Zhangafter returning fromJiameng[葭萌].[Li] Huiknew that[Liu] Zhangwould certainly be defeated and that theFormer Lordwould certainly be triumphant, so he used the pretext of being a commandery messenger to go north to theFormer Lord, chancing upon him atMianzhu[綿竹]. TheFormer Lordadmired him, and from there, they went toLuocheng[洛城], then[Li] Huiwas sent toHanzhong[漢中] to foster friendship withMa Chao.[Ma] Chaothus followed the command [of theFormer Lord].
TheRecords of Huayang Statestates:[Cuan] Xilater reached the rank ofArmy Leader.
WhenChengdu[成都] was stabilized, theFormer Lordled asGovernorofYi province[益州牧], and he had[Li] Huiserve asAssistant Documentator of the Officer of MeritandMaster of Records. Later, he was the subject of false accusations by criminals who were on the run; they implicated[Li] Huiin their rebellious plot. Thedirecting officialsdetained him and sent him off [to theFormer Lord]. TheFormer Lordunderstood that this [the implication] was not so and promoted[Li] HuitoAssistant Official.
In the first year of theera [221], theCommander of Laijiang[庲降都督],Deng Fang, died. TheFormer Lordasked[Li] Hui, “Who can replace him?”[Li] Huireplied, “The talents and capabilities that men possess each have their strengths and weaknesses. This is whyMaster Kongsaid, ‘When he1tasks men, he does so according to their capacity.’ When a wise lord resides in a superior rank, his subordinates should devote themselves to him. Hence, in the war against the Xianlian [Qiang], Zhao Chongguo [趙充國] said, ‘No one can compare to this old minister of yours.’2This humble minister of yours is incapable of evaluating himself well—would thatYour Majestyinvestigate such!” TheFormer Lordchuckled and said, “My first inclination was also on you, sir!” Thus, he had[Li] Huiserve asCommander of Laijiang[庲降都督] and had him lead asInspectorofJiao provincebearing tally[交州刺史] and move toPingyi county[平夷縣].B•••
“He” refers to ajunzi[君子], who is a gentleman and a man of good moral character.↩
Zhao Chongguo [趙充國], styled Wengsun [翁孫], was a military general who served under theHan dynasty. He is best known for his victory over the rebelling Qiang in northwest China. Before that, however, there were doubts if he could lead an army against them. The historical passageLi Huialludes to reads: At the time, [Zhao] Chongguo was over seventy years old. The Sovereign thought him to be too old and sentImperial GrandeeBing Ji to ask, “Who can replace you as a general?” [Zhao] Chongguo replied, “The dead who surpass this old minister of yours.” The Sovereign sent someone to ask him, “General, in your judgment of the Qiang enemies, how many men would you use against them?” [Zhao] Chongguo said, “One hundred reports cannot compare to one observation. I, your minister, am willing to gallop toJinchengand plan excellent means and strategies. Besides, the Qiang barbarians are just petty tribes who are disobeying Heaven in their rebellion. Their destruction will not take long, and I hope that Your Majesty shall entrust that to this old minister of yours. You would not have to worry!” And the Sovereign smiled and said, “This is approved.” [時充國年七十餘，上老之，使御史大夫丙吉問：「誰可將者？」充國對曰：「亡踰於老臣者矣。」上遣問焉，曰：「將軍度羌虜何如，當用幾人？」充國曰：「百聞不如一見。兵難隃度，臣願馳至金城，圖上方略。然羌戎小夷，逆天背畔，滅亡不久，願陛下以屬老臣，勿以為憂。」上笑曰：「諾。」]↩
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, have made inquiries to the people of Shu [蜀]. They have said that there was a region calledLaijiang[庲降], which was over two thousandliaway from Shu [蜀].3At the time,Ning province[寧州] had not existed yet, but it [the area] was called theNanzhong region. This post was established in order to govern it. It was during theera ofJinthat it [the area] was divided to makeNing province[寧州].•••
This is presumably referring to where the government of Shu was located, which would be inChengdu.↩
When theFormer Lorddied,Gao Dingacted without limit inYuexi[越巂],Yong Kaiacted with conceit inJianning[建寧], andZhu Baorebelled inZangke[牂牁]. The,[Zhuge] Liang, went on an expedition against the south. They first went throughYuexi[越巂], then[Li] Huifollowed the roads toJianning[建寧]. The counties there had united and besieged the army of[Li] HuiatKunming[昆明]. At the time,[Li] Huihad few forces and the enemies were twice of his, and he had also not received any news of[Zhuge] Liang. Thus, he tricked the people of the south, saying, “The military has run out of provisions, and so we are seeking to withdraw. I have been away from my home village for some time, and now I may be able to travel back. If I am unable to return to the north, then I intend to return back here so we can plot together. This I tell in sincerity.” The people of the south believed him, so they neglected and relaxed the defenses of their siege.[Li] Huithen marched out to strike them, crushing them, then pursued and destroyed them. To the south, he went to thePan river[槃江], and to the east, he approachedZangke[牂牁]. He and[Zhuge] Liangsupported each other in power and might. The lands of the south were thus pacified, with the army of[Li] Huihaving the majority of the merits. He was enfeoffed asDistrict Marquisof Hanxing [漢興亭侯] and given the title ofGeneral who Secures the Han.
Later, when the army rebelled, the barbarians of the south rebelled once more and killed the administrator.[Li] Huipersonally went to suppress them, exterminating all of those who were wicked and moving their leaders toChengdu[成都]. He taxed from the Sou [叟] and the Pu [濮], their plowing cattle, warhorses, gold and silver, and rhinoceros skin continuously filling military supplies. Thus, at that time, resources and materials were not lacking.
In the seventh year of theera [229],Jiao province[交州] was placed underWu, hence[Li] Huiwas relieved of being itsInspectorand promptly made theGrand AdministratorofJianning[建寧太守], returning to reside in his home commandery. He then moved to reside inHanzhong[漢中].
In the ninth year [231], he died. His son,[Li] Yi, succeeded him. The son of the younger brother of[Li] Hui,[Li] Qiu, served asMarshal of the Right Feathered Forest Units. He followedZhuge Zhanto resistDeng Ai, and in battle, he gave his life, dying atMianzhu[綿竹].
